name: Moncayo Loop: Ágreda and Tarazona
description: This difficult 36.8-mile road cycling route in Parque Natural del Moncayo offers challenging terrain and scenic views for experienced cyclis
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ágreda are defined by the region's position at the foot of the Moncayo massif, offering varied and challenging terrain. The landscape features significant elevation changes, with Ágreda itself at 941 meters and nearby areas reaching higher altitudes. Cyclists encounter ancient forests of oak, beech, and pine, alongside open vistas. The area provides a network of roads suitable for demanding climbs and scenic rides. Best road cycling routes around Ágreda The most popular road cycling route is Palacio del Papa Luna by the Sierra de Moncayo — circular from Ólvega, a 73.3 miles (118.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 43 minutes to complete. This route explores the Sierra de Moncayo, featuring substantial elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Moncayo CIMA Climbs Loop, a difficult 38.6 miles (62.1 km) path. This route includes significant climbs characteristic of the Moncayo region. Local road cyclists also love the Moncayo Loop: Ágreda and Tarazona, a 36.8 miles (59.2 km) trail leading through the Moncayo foothills, often completed in about 3 hours 10 minutes. Road cycling around Ágreda is defined by the Moncayo massif, high elevation roads, and forested sections, offering challenging climbs and scenic views. The network includes options for various ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Ágreda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from 2 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ágreda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ágreda?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes around Ágreda, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. The region is particularly known for its challenging terrain at the foot of the Moncayo massif.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Ágreda?
A: Road cycling routes in Ágreda are characterized by significant elevation changes due to the region's position at the foot of the Moncayo massif. You'll encounter ancient forests of oak, beech, and pine, alongside open vistas and demanding climbs. The roads are generally well-maintained with minimal traffic, providing a pleasant cycling experience.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Ágreda?
A: While Ágreda is known for its challenging terrain, with 18 routes rated as difficult and 8 as moderate, there are no routes specifically classified as easy. Cyclists should be prepared for substantial ascents and descents, making the area more suitable for those with good to very good fitness levels.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Ágreda?
A: Yes, many routes around Ágreda are designed as loops. For instance, you can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1075617240/palacio-del-papa-luna-by-the-sierra-de-moncayo-circular-from-olvega" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palacio del Papa Luna by the Sierra de Moncayo — circular from Ólvega&lt;/a&gt;, which is a substantial 73.3-mile loop, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1075610431/moncayo-cima-climbs-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo CIMA Climbs Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 38.6-mile path featuring significant climbs.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while road cycling near Ágreda?
A: The Moncayo Natural Park is a prominent feature, offering stunning backdrops with ancient forests and clear streams. You can also find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3924334" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Summit&lt;/a&gt;, which provides splendid panoramic views, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5943898" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soriano Natural Water Trail&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features the Cañon del Val and the Nacedero del Río Queiles.

Q: Are there historical or cultural sights to explore along road cycling routes in Ágreda?
A: Absolutely. Ágreda is known as the 'town of the three cultures' with a rich heritage. You can visit Romanesque temples like San Miguel Arcángel, Virgen de la Peña, and San Juan Bautista. The 17th-century Palace of the Castejones and the Arco Árabe are also significant historical attractions. Nearby Tarazona, often included in loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1342101160/moncayo-loop-agreda-and-tarazona" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Loop: Ágreda and Tarazona&lt;/a&gt;, features a Renaissance town hall and a 12th-century French Gothic cathedral.

Q: Are there good viewpoints on the road cycling routes around Ágreda?
A: Yes, the high elevation of Ágreda and its surroundings, particularly the Moncayo massif, offers expansive views. Notable viewpoints include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3924334" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Summit&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5573920" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Tío Fresquito&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt;, which can be incorporated into your rides for breathtaking panoramas.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Ágreda?
A: Given Ágreda's elevation of 941 meters and nearby Cueva de Ágreda at 1,301 meters, the region experiences cooler weather. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the colder temperatures of winter, especially at higher altitudes in the Moncayo Natural Park.

Q: Is there parking available for road cyclists in Ágreda?
A: While specific parking areas for cyclists are not detailed, Ágreda and surrounding towns like Ólvega, which serve as starting points for routes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31844091" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Borobia – Cueva de Ágreda loop from Ólvega&lt;/a&gt;, typically offer public parking facilities. It's advisable to check local parking options upon arrival.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Ágreda?
A: The road cycling routes in Ágreda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from 2 reviews. Over 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the varied and challenging terrain, often praising the significant climbs and scenic views offered by the Moncayo region.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay for cyclists in Ágreda?
A: Yes, Ágreda and its surroundings offer options for cyclists. You can find places like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3924337" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santuario del Moncayo Restaurant and Hostel&lt;/a&gt;, which provides both dining and accommodation. Additionally, the region has various cafes, bars, and accommodations in towns like Ágreda and nearby Tarazona that cater to visitors.
tips: Population that is more than nine hundred meters high and that is flagged by its castle, the Castillo de Vozmediano. Its two towers watch over the small valley in which the town is located.

We are at the foot of the imposing windmills near the town of Grisel. We will have spectacular views of Moncayo and the Tarazona plain and other mountains that we can see on the information/geographic panel.

Visiting it is doing a deep review of the history of art in Spain, since in its construction there are spaces where all the styles that have existed since the 12th century, the time when construction began, come together.  It is a unique example of a Gothic cathedral with an exceptional Mudejar and Renaissance legacy.

The Tarazona Cathedral was built in the French Gothic style, but it maintains magnificent examples of the Mudejar style, including a Gothic-Mudejar cloister.

Beautiful town of Los Fayos which is surrounded by two rivers, one of which passes through the same town, eñ Río Queiles which is the main one. The Embalse de Val dam is nearby as is the spectacular Castle that is built inside a cave and can be seen from the road.
Ágreda gives its name to what is called Tierra de Ágreda and which represent the set of populations that are found together in the valley where the Río Añamaza passes. Nice town to take a break.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: The circular route typically starts from Ágreda. You should be able to find parking within the town to begin your journey.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through natural areas and towns, specific regulations for dogs are not detailed. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash, especially in populated areas or near wildlife, and to check local signage for any restrictions within the Parque Natural del Moncayo or specific towns.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging terrain and potential for varied weather in the Moncayo region, spring and autumn are generally the best times. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring snow or very cold conditions, especially at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail, especially within Parque Natural del Moncayo?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or access the Parque Natural del Moncayo. However, always respect local regulations and private property. Some specific activities or areas within the park might have their own rules, so it's wise to check if you plan any detours.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route features a very diverse and often demanding terrain. Only about 19% is asphalt. A significant portion, approximately 53%, is unpaved, with about 46% specifically identified as gravel. You'll also encounter 'track' sections making up 55% of the route. This means you'll experience a mix of surfaces, with substantial gravel and unpaved sections that can be challenging.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Ágreda, Vozmediano y Tarazona — circular route for cycling?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult and demands very good fitness. The challenge comes from the varied and often rough surfaces, including extensive gravel, combined with notable elevation changes (around 960 meters up). You might find yourself walking your bike in some of the more difficult unpaved segments.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll pass through several historic towns and natural highlights. Key points include the Castillo de Vozmediano, the Mirador de La Diezma Viewpoint, the impressive Cathedral of Saint Mary of the Huerta of Tarazona, and the Castle Cave. The town of Ágreda itself is also a highlight.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other notable trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that are part of the [CIMA ZA05] Moncayo * Tarazona, Camino Natural del Agua Soriano. Camino Antonino, [CIMA ZA05] Moncayo * Vozmediano, Camino de la Vera Cruz (El camino de los Templarios), and the Camino Jacobeo Castellano-Aragonés: Tarazona-Ágreda, among others.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for a traditional racebike?
faqs.answer: While listed as a 'racebike' route, its suitability for traditional road cycling with thin tires is questionable. A substantial portion (around 53%, with 46% being gravel) is unpaved. You may encounter difficult conditions where tires could slip, potentially requiring you to walk your bike in some segments. A gravel bike or mountain bike would likely be more appropriate for comfort and safety.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and natural features will I encounter?
faqs.answer: You'll traverse picturesque landscapes influenced by the majestic Moncayo mountain range. The route passes through historic towns like Ágreda, Vozmediano, and Tarazona, and includes areas like Aldehuela de Ágreda, a mountain village. You'll also see the Queiles River, and experience the wild, sparsely populated natural surroundings associated with the 'Montañas Vacías' (Empty Mountains) concept.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several towns, including Ágreda, Vozmediano, and Tarazona. These towns offer opportunities to find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation, especially in the larger centers like Ágreda and Tarazona. It's a good idea to plan your stops in advance, especially in smaller villages.

waypoints.name: Cathedral of Saint Mary of the Huerta of Tarazona
